items.title: We’ve identified industrial-scale distillation attacks on our models by DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and M
items.raw_summary:
We’ve identified industrial-scale distillation attacks on our models by DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax.

These labs created over 24,000 fraudulent accounts and generated over 16 million exchanges with Claude, extracting its capabilities to train and improve their own models.
items.why_it_matters: Anthropic publicly accuses DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax of industrial-scale model distillation via 24,000+ fake accounts — a major escalation in AI IP protection.
items.action: Read Anthropic's distillation attack report

items.title: The standard for frontier coding evals is changing with model maturity.
items.raw_summary:
The standard for frontier coding evals is changing with model maturity.

We now recommend reporting SWE-bench Pro and are sharing more detail on why we’re no longer reporting SWE-bench Verified as we work with the industry to establish stronger coding eval standards.

SWE-bench V
items.why_it_matters: OpenAI formally recommends SWE-bench Pro over SWE-bench Verified, signaling that frontier coding evals need higher bars as models mature.
items.action: Switch to SWE-bench Pro for model comparisons

items.title: New research: The AI Fluency Index.
items.raw_summary:
New research: The AI Fluency Index.

We tracked 11 behaviors across thousands of https://t.co/RxKnLNNcNR conversations—for example, how often people iterate and refine their work with Claude—to measure how well people collaborate with AI.

Read more: https://t.co/g65nGQFmjG
items.why_it_matters: Anthropic's AI Fluency Index quantifies 11 collaboration behaviors across thousands of conversations, providing the first structured framework for measuring human-AI interaction quality.
items.action: Benchmark your team's AI collaboration patterns

items.title: 28 Days of Claude API - Day 23 - Fine-Grained Tool Streaming
items.raw_summary:
28 Days of Claude API - Day 23 - Fine-Grained Tool Streaming

Streaming text often feels instant. Tool calls don't.

That's because tool parameters buffer until the JSON validates before they stream to you.

"eager_input_streaming: true" removes that wait.
items.why_it_matters: Eager input streaming for tool calls removes the JSON validation buffer delay, making agentic UIs feel significantly more responsive.
items.action: Enable eager_input_streaming in tool-heavy apps

items.title: 🚨BREAKING: Google DeepMind + Meta + Amazon just dropped a 100 page roadmap that redefines what AI a
items.raw_summary:
🚨BREAKING: Google DeepMind + Meta + Amazon just dropped a 100 page roadmap that redefines what AI agents actually are.

They analyzed every major agentic reasoning framework across planning, tool use, memory, and multi-agent coordination.

Single-agent in stable environments: works great.
Multi-agent in dynamic environments: falls apart.

The problem isn't intelligence. It's how agents reason under uncertainty.

Three layers they found that actually matter:

→ Foundational reasoning (planning +
items.why_it_matters: A joint Google DeepMind + Meta + Amazon 100-page report finds multi-agent systems fail in dynamic environments — the bottleneck is reasoning architecture, not intelligence.
items.action: Read the agentic reasoning framework analysis

items.title: 🎨 Generating or editing images at scale?
items.raw_summary:
🎨 Generating or editing images at scale?

Batch API now supports GPT Image models: gpt-image-1.5, chatgpt-image-latest, gpt-image-1, gpt-image-1-mini.

Submit up to 50,000 generations or edits as async jobs at 50% lower cost with separate rate limits.

https://t.co/9W6TCeBGw3 ht
items.why_it_matters: OpenAI Batch API now supports all GPT Image models at 50% lower cost with 50K async job limits, making large-scale image generation economically viable.
items.action: Migrate bulk image generation to Batch API

items.title: unpopular (maybe?) opinion: MCP is dead in the water
items.raw_summary:
unpopular (maybe?) opinion: MCP is dead in the water

@openclaw has shown me that api & cli will win. 

every MCP server you connect loads its tool definitions into your context window. name, description, parameter schema, all of it. connect 10 servers with 5 tools each and you've burned 50 tool definitions worth of tokens before your conversation even starts.

context bloat will never be a good thing - performance-wise or economically. i assume this is why @steipete left it out of @openclaw.

t
items.why_it_matters: A practitioner argues MCP is fundamentally flawed because loading 50+ tool definitions burns context before conversations start — context bloat may favor CLI/API approaches.
items.action: Audit your MCP tool count and context usage

items.title: > be Sammy Azdoufal, software engineer
items.raw_summary:
> be Sammy Azdoufal, software engineer
> spend $2000 on DJI Romo vacuum
> decide to control it with xbox controller like a chad
> use Claude to reverse engineer the API
> It works because Claude is the GOAT
> just need to grab auth token from their cloud servers
> token works... Claude is unbeaten 
> wait why is he authenticated as 7000 devices
> ohno.jpg
> backend trusted any valid token for any device, no ownership verification
> mfw Sammy has live camera feeds from vacuums in 24 countries
> w
items.why_it_matters: A developer reverse-engineering a DJI vacuum API accidentally gained access to 7,000 devices due to a backend auth flaw — a cautionary tale about IoT trust boundaries.
items.action: Audit your API auth token scoping

items.title: Tiny (4GB) open source LLM models already match GPT 4.0. You can download one and run it for free on
items.raw_summary: Tiny (4GB) open source LLM models already match GPT 4.0. You can download one and run it for free on your entry-level GPU (runs on iGPU too). If these small open source models are good enough for most consumers, they will never become paying customers. That's a big risk.
items.why_it_matters: 4GB open-source models now match GPT-4.0 on consumer hardware including iGPUs — a potential threat to paid AI subscription models as local inference becomes good enough.
items.action: Test small local models against your use cases

items.title: Software Engineer position will never die
items.raw_summary:
Imagine your boss pays you $570,000. Then tells the world your job disappears in 6 months.

That just happened at Anthropic.

Dario Amodei told Davos that Al can handle "most, maybe all" coding tasks in 6 to 12 months. His own engineers don't write code anymore. They edit what Al produces.

Meanwhile, Anthropic pays senior engineers a median of $570k. Some roles hit $759k. L5/L6 postings confirm $474k to $615k.

They're still hiring.

The $570k engineers aren't writing for loops. They decide whi
items.why_it_matters: The tension between Anthropic paying engineers $570K+ while its CEO says AI replaces coding in 6-12 months crystallizes the industry's contradictory signals about SWE roles.
items.action: Focus on AI-augmented workflows, not replacement anxiety
